citation: Dupont MM, Fourman MS, Iyer S, Qureshi SA, Sheha ED, Rhie-Lee J, Dowdell J. Impact of lumbar disk herniation on performance outcomes and new contracts in the National Football League. Clin Spine Surg. 2022;00:000-000.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined 101 NFL players treated for lumbar disk herniation (LDH) from 2000 to 2020 using public records, Pro Football Focus performance grades, return-to-play measures, and contract data. Seventy-five players (74.3%) returned to at least one NFL game after treatment. Posttreatment PFF grade was not significantly different from preinjury grade, but total and guaranteed contract values decreased significantly after LDH treatment. Lower age and higher preinjury PFF grade independently predicted both return to play and signing a new contract, while treatment type was not independently associated with these outcomes.
tldr: Most NFL players with LDH returned and performed near baseline, but their market value dropped; age, preinjury performance, and prior contract status mattered more than operative versus nonoperative treatment.
methods: Retrospective cohort of NFL players treated for LDH from 2000-2020 identified through public records. Collected age, BMI, position, NFL experience, treatment type, RTP timing, games played/started, PFF grades, and contract values. PFF grade and contract values were compared before and after treatment. Logistic regression tested predictors of RTP, signing a new contract, and signing a posttreatment contract with more than 20% guaranteed money.
findings:
- 101 NFL players were identified; median age was 26.9 years and median NFL experience was 4.5 years.
- 77 players required surgery and 24 were managed nonoperatively.
- RTP rate was 75/101 (74.3%).
- Nonoperative players who returned did so faster than surgical players: median 1.8 vs 7.9 months.
- Mean PFF grade was similar before vs after treatment: 67.9 vs 65.6, p = 0.2.
- Median total annual contract value dropped from $5.97M to $2.00M; median guaranteed value dropped from $1.67M to $0.47M; both p < 0.01.
- Younger age and higher preinjury PFF grade independently predicted RTP and ability to sign a new contract.
- Higher preinjury guaranteed-to-total contract ratio predicted signing a posttreatment contract with more than 20% guaranteed money.
limitations: Public-record injury identification may miss undisclosed LDH cases, retired players, and nonoperative cases; diagnosis and treatment details were incomplete; PFF grades are human-coded and may contain analyst bias; injury specifics such as free fragments were not captured; retrospective observational design cannot establish causal effects of treatment choice.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, LDH counseling should separate medical recovery from roster/market consequences. A player can return and perform well yet still face reduced perceived durability or availability. Track preinjury performance, age/training age, positional demands, and contract/scholarship implications when framing expectations. Treatment choice alone should not be oversold as the determinant of football success.
athleteDev: Spine health programming should emphasize progressive trunk capacity, hip strength, posterior-chain tolerance, landing/contact mechanics, and monitored heavy lifting exposure. For high-value contributors, early objective baselines for strength, movement, symptoms, and football function can help staff distinguish true performance decline from conservative decision-making after injury.
rtp: RTP rate was 74.3%, with preserved average PFF performance among those with available grades. Nonoperative return was faster among returners, but surgery was not independently linked to worse RTP or contract outcomes. RTP decisions should use symptoms, neurologic status, position-specific workload tolerance, and repeated football exposure rather than assuming procedure type determines readiness.

citation: Nassis GP, Girard O, Chiampas GT, Krustrup P, Racinais S. In-match strategies to mitigate the effect of heat on football (soccer) players' health and performance. Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-2.
abstract: This editorial summarizes evidence-based in-match strategies to reduce heat strain during football played in hot environments, with particular attention to the 2026 FIFA World Cup host climates. Projected afternoon WBGT values of 30-35 degrees C in several host cities create meaningful player health and performance challenges. The authors recommend heat acclimation/acclimatization, hydration, cooling breaks, modified warm-ups, tactical pacing, cold-water immersion at halftime when practical, ice towels, chilled fluids, head/hand/forearm cooling, fan or skin-wetting approaches, and inclusion of medical personnel in scheduling and safety decisions.
tldr: In hot matches, the best protection starts before kickoff with acclimation and hydration, then uses practical cooling at halftime and breaks to keep core temperature, skin temperature, perception, and cognition under control.
methods: Narrative editorial drawing from consensus statements and realistic football or intermittent-exercise heat studies. The paper frames expected 2026 World Cup heat exposure, explains thermoregulatory and cognitive risks, and translates available cooling evidence into in-match recommendations for medical and support staff.
findings:
- WBGT may reach 30-35 degrees C in several 2026 host cities during afternoon windows.
- Football in heat can produce rectal temperatures of 39.5-40.2 degrees C and muscle temperatures up to 42 degrees C.
- Complex cognitive performance begins to decline above core temperatures around 38.7 degrees C.
- Recommended targets include core temperature below 39 degrees C, skin temperature below 33 degrees C, and quadriceps temperature around 39 degrees C.
- Cold-water immersion is the most effective halftime cooling option, dropping internal temperature about 0.25-0.35 degrees C per minute.
- Large-surface ice towels are the next practical option at about 0.1 degrees C per minute.
- Three-minute cooling breaks are too short for large core-temperature changes, so staff should prioritize hydration, thermal sensation, skin cooling, and cognitive preservation.
limitations: Editorial rather than primary trial; most evidence is from male players or non-football protocols; direct evidence on football decision-making under heat is limited; feasibility varies by venue, locker-room access, competition rules, equipment, staffing, and match context; more work is needed in women, youth, referees, extra time, and repeated tournament matches.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, heat planning should be operational: acclimation blocks, individualized hydration plans, cold towels/ice slush/chilled fluids, shaded benches, fans or misting, and rehearsed halftime cooling routines. Short stoppages should focus on perception and skin cooling, while longer breaks can target core temperature. Medical staff should have authority in heat-risk decisions, especially during camp, early-season games, and travel.
athleteDev: Heat tolerance is trainable. Build acclimation over 10-14 sessions when possible, monitor body mass changes and symptoms, and teach athletes to use cooling resources quickly without disrupting tactical communication. Skill and decision-making periods in heat should be protected because cognitive strain may rise before obvious performance collapse.
rtp: Not an RTP study, but relevant to heat illness return and same-game availability. Athletes with heat symptoms should be removed, cooled aggressively, and medically assessed rather than managed only with hydration. Return to participation after exertional heat illness should require symptom resolution, graded heat re-exposure, and medical clearance.

citation: Battiste CI IV, Danoff CJ, Hanes C. Lending a Hand in Bringing Ballcarriers Down: An Extended Study of Individual Defensive Contributions in the National Football League (NFL). SN Comput Sci. 2025;6:622.
abstract: This sports analytics study used 2024 NFL Big Data Bowl player-tracking data from the 2022 NFL season, weeks 1-9, to quantify individual defensive influence on ballcarrier deceleration. The authors created a 'slowdown' metric using ballcarrier speed, defender proximity, distance matrices, play frames, and tackle data. Slowdowns correlated strongly with tackles and assists, and defender distance significantly predicted ballcarrier speed. In multivariable models, each additional defender within 5 yards was associated with lower ballcarrier speed, supporting the football principle that nearby pursuit and swarm behavior matter even when a defender does not record the tackle.
tldr: Tracking data can credit defenders for slowing ballcarriers before the tackle; proximity and number of nearby defenders meaningfully reduce ballcarrier speed and reveal off-ball defensive value.
methods: Used NFL Next Gen Stats and PFF data from the 2024 NFL Big Data Bowl, covering 136 games, 12,483 plays, 1,683 players, 480 unique ballcarriers, and frame-level tracking data from weeks 1-9 of the 2022 season. Data were cleaned in Python/pandas and modeled with SQL-backed tables. Euclidean distance matrices measured each defender's distance to the ballcarrier in each frame. Slowdown credit was assigned from ballcarrier deceleration and nearest-defender influence. Correlations and OLS regression tested relationships among distance, number of nearby defenders, play type, missed tackles, frame ID, and ballcarrier speed.
findings:
- Top accumulated slowdown players were all linebackers, led by C.J. Mosley at 691.02 mph of summed slowdowns through weeks 1-9.
- Slowdowns correlated strongly with individual and assisted tackles, with reported correlations around 0.82-0.92.
- Defender distance was a significant positive predictor of ballcarrier speed: as defenders were farther away, ballcarriers moved faster.
- OLS coefficients for distance to ballcarrier were significant across cutoffs: <1 yard 0.7537, <2 yards 0.7099, <3 yards 0.6063, <4 yards 0.5056; all p < 0.001.
- In a multivariable model restricted to closest defender within 2.5 yards, R2 = 0.401.
- Each additional defender within 5 yards was associated with -0.6608 yards/sec ballcarrier speed, p < 0.001.
- Missed tackles were associated with +0.5191 yards/sec speed, and scrambles had higher speed than completions.
limitations: Observational tracking analysis from only the first nine weeks of one NFL season; slowdown attribution depends on assumptions about nearest-defender responsibility and ballcarrier deceleration; context such as play call, assignment, leverage, fatigue, injury status, field conditions, and opponent quality is only partially captured; results should supplement, not replace, film grading and coaching evaluation.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, a slowdown-style metric could add value to defensive grading by crediting pursuit, leverage, funneling, contain, and swarm behaviors that do not appear in the tackle column. It can help identify linebackers, safeties, edges, and nickel defenders who repeatedly reduce space and create tackle opportunities for teammates.
athleteDev: Development staff can use the concept to coach pursuit angles, closing speed, leverage, and effort away from the ball. Position groups should be compared within role, not globally, because linebackers naturally accumulate more opportunities. Pairing GPS/LPS tracking with film tags could create a practical college version for weekly defensive accountability.
rtp: Not an RTP paper. A related application is return-to-performance after defensive lower-extremity injury: beyond top speed, staff could monitor whether a defender regains preinjury proximity behavior, pursuit involvement, and capacity to close space during live or simulated play.

citation: Littleford K, Arbon JJ, DeFoor MT, Cognetti DJ. Medical Malpractice Lawsuits Against Team Physicians in the National Football League (NFL) Increased Between 1971 and 2020 But Remain Low Overall. Arthrosc Sports Med Rehabil. 2024;101013.
abstract: This study reviewed publicly available legal databases, court records, and news archives to identify medical malpractice lawsuits involving NFL players and team physicians from 1971 to 2020. Across 273 identified team physicians, including 190 orthopaedic surgeons, the authors found 28 lawsuits by current or former NFL players. Lawsuits increased significantly over time (p = 0.0086, R2 = 0.60), rising from 0.28 per year in 1971-1995 to 0.84 per year in 1996-2020, but remained low in absolute number. Knee, spine, and foot/ankle injuries were the most common categories, with additional cases involving heat-related death, medication prescribing practices, and anti-inflammatory use.
tldr: NFL medical malpractice lawsuits against team physicians are uncommon but increasing, with knee, spine, foot/ankle, heat illness, and medication decisions carrying notable legal and communication risk.
methods: Two independent reviewers searched public legal databases, court records, news archives, and Google in August 2023. Searches combined each NFL team name with 'medical malpractice' or 'lawsuit,' then searched associated orthopaedic team physicians by physician name, team, malpractice, and lawsuit terms. Included medical malpractice claims by NFL players; excluded non-player plaintiffs and non-medical lawsuits. Outcomes included lawsuit count, physician specialty, allegations, body region, monetary amounts, and disposition when available. Linear regression tested lawsuit trends over time.
findings:
- 273 NFL-associated physicians were identified; 190 were orthopaedic surgeons.
- 28 lawsuits from current or former NFL players were identified from 1971-2020.
- Lawsuit rate rose from 7 cases in 1971-1995 (0.28/year) to 21 cases in 1996-2020 (0.84/year).
- Linear regression showed a significant increase over time: p = 0.0086, R2 = 0.60.
- Most common body areas were knee (n = 11), spine (n = 4), and foot/ankle (n = 4), followed by shoulder, wrist, hip, lung, and other categories.
- Some cases involved premature RTP clearance, failure to diagnose, lack of informed consent, medication prescribing, anti-inflammatory use, and wrongful death from heat-related injury.
- Highest filed amount was $180M, and at least six lawsuits exceeded $1M, though many outcomes were sealed or unavailable.
limitations: Reliance on public and often non-peer-reviewed sources; sealed records, appeals, settlements, and incomplete online records limited outcome ascertainment; older lawsuits may be undercounted due to pre-internet availability, creating possible recency bias; analysis counts known lawsuits rather than true malpractice incidence or quality of care.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the main lesson is process quality: clear informed consent, defensible RTP documentation, transparent communication, medication governance, and athlete-centered decision-making reduce risk while improving care. High-risk areas include knee surgery, spine decisions, foot/ankle diagnosis, concussion, heat illness, and pain-medication practices.
athleteDev: Athlete development depends on trust. Medical, performance, coaching, family, and agent communication should be structured so the athlete understands diagnosis, options, uncertainty, and consequences of playing through injury. Documentation should reflect the athlete's goals and medical rationale, not only availability pressure.
rtp: Several lawsuits centered on alleged premature clearance or failure to disclose injury severity. RTP systems should require objective criteria, documented risk discussion, and physician independence, especially for spine, knee ligament/meniscus, concussion, heat illness, and cases where competitive or financial incentives may pressure the athlete.

citation: Tenan MS. Missing Data in Sport Science: A Didactic Example Using Wearables in American Football. Sports Med. 2023.
abstract: This leading article used Oura Ring sleep data from a collegiate American football program to demonstrate how missing wearable data can bias sport science dashboards. During the 2021 season, 109 athletes received Oura Rings; 75 athletes with at least 66% recorded data were analyzed from August 7 to November 27. Little's test and multilevel logistic regression showed the missing completely at random assumption was violated: missingness changed across time, player position, and day of week. The author compared deletion with 11 imputation methods and showed that common 7-day moving averages using pairwise deletion can diverge from imputed estimates, potentially misleading practitioners.
tldr: Wearable data missingness in football is not harmless; deleting missing values can bias dashboards, so teams should test missingness patterns and use appropriate imputation before making readiness or recovery decisions.
methods: Case study of 2021 West Virginia University football Oura Ring sleep data. Analyzed 75 college-age male athletes with at least 66% data recorded, yielding 7,188 non-missing and 1,212 missing data points. Tested MCAR with Little's test and a multilevel logistic missingness model using athlete random intercepts plus day number, day of week, and position. Compared pairwise deletion against mean, median, last-observation-carried-forward, random, moving average, weighted moving average, exponentially weighted moving average, linear interpolation, spline, Stineman interpolation, and multilevel multiple imputation. Sequential real-time imputation began 7 days into the season and was evaluated against end-of-season imputation using mean absolute error.
findings:
- Little's test indicated violation of the MCAR assumption: p < 0.001.
- Day number, day of week, and player position all significantly predicted missingness: p < 0.01.
- Response rate decreased across the season.
- Missingness varied by position; cornerback vs tight end/fullback differed in post-hoc testing.
- Missingness differed strongly by day of week, especially Saturday/Sunday versus other days and Monday/Friday versus Tuesday-Thursday.
- Moving-average imputation approaches showed low bias and slope across serial real-time calculations.
- Multiple imputation became more stable as data accumulated, reaching wide-sense stability by about Day 50.
- Standard 7-day moving averages with pairwise deletion were clear outliers compared with most imputation approaches.
limitations: Single collegiate football program and one wearable/sleep metric; exact athlete ages not recorded; the true data-generating mechanism for missingness is unknown, so imputed values cannot be proven correct; imputation methods were compared against their own end-of-season estimates rather than ground truth; default package settings may not be optimal for every team or metric.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, dashboards should flag expected-but-missing data, test whether missingness is related to weekday, travel, position, depth chart, injury, or time in season, and avoid treating deletion as neutral. Readiness, sleep, GPS, HRV, and wellness reports should include missingness diagnostics and imputation rules reviewed by sport science, athletic training, and coaching stakeholders.
athleteDev: Athletes with low compliance may not be random: missing data may reflect fatigue, travel, injury, dissatisfaction, technology friction, or role status. Development plans should include education, device support, and athlete-specific compliance tracking so staff do not mistake cleaner data from compliant athletes for better recovery or adaptation.
rtp: Not an RTP study, but directly relevant to RTP dashboards. If an injured athlete has missing sleep, workload, or wellness data during rehab, deleting those values can falsely reassure staff. RTP monitoring should explicitly document missingness, use defensible imputation or conservative interpretation, and avoid clearance decisions based on biased data summaries.
